images.subject: The front page of The New York Times from Wednesday, November 10, 1965. The main headline reads: 'POWER FAILURE SNARLS NORTHEAST; 800,000 ARE CAUGHT IN SUBWAYS HERE; AUTOS TIED UP, CITY GROPES IN DARK'. The page includes the newspaper's masthead, various sub-headlines and articles, and a small, grainy, high-contrast photograph of a city skyline (likely New York City) during the blackout, showing dark buildings against a lighter sky.
images.composition: A direct, high-contrast reproduction of a newspaper front page. The composition is dense with text arranged in columns. A block of new text is overlaid on the bottom third of the page, obscuring some of the original newspaper content. The image has a grainy, slightly distorted quality characteristic of halftone printing and reproduction.

design.layout_description: This is a single-page layout, not a spread. The entire page is filled with a reproduction of a newspaper front page. A rectangular block of text is superimposed over the bottom third of the newspaper image, creating a layered effect where the new text comments on the background image.

typography.body_font_style: The overlaid text is a sans-serif font, medium weight, set in a justified block.
typography.display_font_style: The newspaper features multiple display styles: the iconic blackletter (Gothic) masthead for 'The New York Times', and large, bold, condensed sans-serif fonts for the headlines.

color_and_tone.description: The page is entirely black and white, mimicking the original newspaper. The contrast is stark between the black ink and the off-white paper background.

spread_description: The reader is confronted with the historic front page of The New York Times reporting on the 1965 Northeast blackout, a moment when the electric environment suddenly failed. Superimposed over this document of technological collapse is McLuhan's text, which uses the blackout as a concrete example of how 'electric technology shapes, works over, alters—massages—every instant of our lives.' The design forces the reader to see the historical event through McLuhan's theoretical lens, re-framing a news story as evidence for his central argument.
